13.display: Wrong — the angle is found by dividing the frequency by the total, then multiplying by 360°, i.e. $(\text{frequency}/\text{total}) \times 360°$.

14.display: True — every row total and every column total in a two-way table must sum to the same overall total (the grand total).

15.display: A vertical line chart uses thin lines (not wide bars) to show frequencies for discrete numerical values, while a bar chart typically uses wider bars, often for categorical data.

16.display: Always — a time series graph is, by definition, used to show how a quantity changes over time.

17.display: True — grouping data into class intervals means you can no longer see each individual value, only which range it falls into (e.g. you know a height is between 140 and 150 cm, but not the exact value).

18.display: If intervals overlapped, a value could fit into more than one class, causing confusion or double-counting when building the frequency table.

19.display: A bar chart has a numbered vertical axis, so you can read the exact frequency for each bar, whereas a pie chart only shows the proportion of the whole, making exact values harder to read without extra information.

20.display: Follow the inequality signs exactly — since the interval is written $140 \leq h < 150$, a value of exactly 140 is included in that interval (because of the $\leq$), not the one below it.

22.display: 32 play an instrument; 20 girls in total

24.display: An overall increasing trend in sales from £300 to £400, with small dips in a couple of months.

26.display: 25 pupils in total; mode is 1 sibling

29.display: Your own grouped frequency table with 2-second class intervals from 12 seconds, frequencies summing to 40, and the correct modal class identified.

30.display: An overall upward trend in profits, apart from a dip in year 2; a limitation is that 5 data points are not enough to reliably predict future profits, since trends could change due to unseen factors.
